_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 9-18R Ceftriaxone (Revised 8/11/2023, MDHHS Reviewed 2023). Base Michigan template.
protocol_reference: Michigan Medication Protocol Section 9-18R: Ceftriaxone — MDHHS Approved 8/11/2023, Reviewed 2023
indications: Major soft tissue injuries with contamination risk
Alternative to cefazolin when cefazolin is unavailable or contraindicated
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to cefepime or cefotaxime (cross-reactivity)
Age <2 months (neonatal contraindication — risk of bilirubin displacement)
Infusion (100 mL NS) CONTRAINDICATED for patients <7 years old
precautions: Do NOT give to patients <2 months of age
Infusion (100 mL NS over 15-30 minutes) is contraindicated for patients <7 years — use slow IVP instead
Confirm allergy history; cross-reactivity with penicillin is low but possible
Reconstitute per kit instructions before administering
expected_effects: Prophylactic antimicrobial coverage for open injuries — broader gram-negative coverage than cefazolin
notes: Dilution for slow IVP: reconstitute 2 grams in 20 mL NS = 100 mg/mL solution
Adult administration: 2 grams slow IVP over 3-5 minutes OR infusion in 100 mL NS over 15-30 minutes
Pediatric >2 months and <7 years: 50 mg/kg max 2 grams slow IVP ONLY
Pediatric ≥7 years: 50 mg/kg max 2 grams; slow IVP or infusion permitted

dosing.dose: 2 grams slow IVP over 3-5 minutes OR 2 grams in 100 mL NS infusion over 15-30 minutes
dosing.notes: Reconstitute: 2 g in 20 mL NS = 100 mg/mL

dosing.dose: 50 mg/kg; maximum 2 grams; slow IVP or infusion permitted
50 mg/kg; maximum 2 grams; slow IVP ONLY
dosing.notes: Infusion contraindicated <7 years; DO NOT give to patients <2 months

used_in_protocols: Trauma — Open Fracture / Amputation / Major Soft Tissue Injury
